does: This paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy of jailbreak attacks against Large Audio Language Models, organizing them into four categories: semantic attacks that manipulate textual meaning, acoustic attacks that exploit speech properties, signal-level attacks using artifacts, and embedding-layer attacks targeting internal representations. It evaluates representative attacks and defenses across ten open-source LALMs, measuring attack success rates alongside practical metrics like benign refusal rates and latency. The key empirical finding is that Acoustic Best-of-N reveals strong worst-case vulnerabilities in audio space while Narrative Framing demonstrates effective low-latency semantic threats, and that current defenses face fundamental tradeoffs between robustness and benign performance.
connects: This work extends jailbreak research from text-only language models to the multimodal setting of audio-language models, building on prior token-level prompting attacks like prompt injection and adversarial examples. It synthesizes heterogeneous threat models and evaluation protocols from earlier LALM safety studies into a unified framework, responding to the lack of standardized comparison across attacks and defenses. The paper combines insights from adversarial machine learning, audio processing, and multimodal learning, connecting to prior work on robustness in speech recognition, cross-modal vulnerabilities, and defense mechanisms for language models.
enables: This work establishes foundations for more rigorous LALM safety research by providing standardized taxonomies and evaluation benchmarks that future work can build upon. It identifies specific vulnerabilities—particularly in acoustic and narrative-based attacks—that point toward developing more sophisticated audio-native defenses beyond current guard-based approaches. The cost-aware evaluation framework enables researchers to assess defenses not just for robustness but for practical deployment constraints, potentially spurring development of efficient defense mechanisms. Additionally, the characterization of multimodal jailbreak vectors suggests future work in understanding cross-modal interaction in safety and designing defenses that protect the entire perception-to-reasoning pipeline rather than isolated components.
